intro.kicker: Topic 13 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Apparent weight is the reading of a weighing machine that supports a body. When the lift accelerates upward, apparent weight R = m(g + a) > mg; when it accelerates downward at a, R = m(g − a); in free fall (a = g downward), R = 0 (weightlessness). This topic is one of the most consistently tested application problems in NEET’s Newton’s Laws questions.

expectedQuestions.description: Almost guaranteed 4 marks per paper. NEET tests both numerical calculation (find R for given a) and conceptual identification (in which case is apparent weight less than, equal to, or greater than actual weight).

timeRequired.description: 40 min to memorise the 7-case table + 50 min solving numerical problems. The table must be completely internalised because NEET questions give one condition and ask for R or its relationship to mg.

difficulty.description: Formula-direct once the 7 cases are memorised. Common mistake: confusing the sign of acceleration (up vs down). Practice drawing the free body diagram for each case to fix the sign correctly.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers elevator problems in Newton’s Second Law. However NEET explicitly uses a 7-case table format that students should memorise, whereas AP Physics derives results from free body diagrams case by case. NEET MCQs reward memorisation of all 7 cases.

weightage.sectionTitle: Apparent Weight in a Lift — NEET Weightage (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears every year. Often paired with Atwood machine or inclined plane questions.
Most common question type: given acceleration direction and value, find R or compare R with mg.
Free fall (R = 0) and over-deceleration (R < 0 — physically impossible) are recurring concepts.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the 7-Case Table
strategySteps.description: NCERT Table 4.2 has 7 conditions: (1) rest → R = mg; (2) constant velocity (up or down) → R = mg; (3) upward acceleration a → R = m(g+a); (4) a = g upward → R = 2mg; (5) downward acceleration a → R = m(g−a); (6) a = g downward (free fall) → R = 0; (7) a > g downward → R is negative (physically impossible without seat belt). Memorise ALL 7.

strategySteps.title: Fix Sign Convention via FBD
strategySteps.description: Always draw the free body diagram taking upward as positive. Net force = ma = R − mg. Therefore R = m(g + a) for upward a (a is positive), and R = m(g − a) for downward a (a substituted as positive magnitude).

strategySteps.title: Connect to Weightlessness
strategySteps.description: When R = 0, object is in a state of weightlessness but its actual mass and weight (mg) are unchanged. This is the same condition astronauts experience in orbit. NEET MCQs test the distinction between 'weightlessness' and 'zero gravity'.

cards.title: Apparent Weight Complete Notes
cards.description: Complete notes: FBD derivation for all 7 lift conditions, NCERT Table 4.2 with formulas, weightlessness concept, and 12 solved NEET numerical problems with directional sign convention guide.

cards.title: 7-Case Quick Reference Card
cards.description: Laminated-style one-page table of all 7 Cases: condition, FBD diagram reference, formula, and value of R. The fastest revision tool for exam day.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Apparent Weight in a Lift

subtopics.name: Concept of Apparent Weight vs Actual Weight

cards.title: General Formula and Sign Rule
cards.coreRevision: R = m(g + a_eff) where a_eff = +a if lift accelerates upward, a_eff = −a if accelerates downward. Take upward positive; net force = R − mg = m×(net a). So R = mg + ma = m(g+a) for upward acceleration.
points: Lift at rest or uniform velocity: R = mg (no net acceleration)
Lift accelerates up at a: R = m(g+a) > mg (feels heavier)
Lift accelerates down at a: R = m(g−a) < mg (feels lighter)
Free fall (cable snaps): a = g downward, R = m(g−g) = 0 (weightlessness)
Deceleration while moving up = acceleration downward: R = m(g−a)
cards.example: A 60 kg person stands on a scale in a lift accelerating downward at 2 m/s². g = 10 m/s². R = 60(10−2) = 60×8 = 480 N. Scale reads 48 kgf (less than actual 60 kgf).

cards.title: All 7 Cases Summary
cards.coreRevision: Complete 7-case table to memorise for NEET.
points: Case 1: Lift at rest → R = mg
Case 2: Constant velocity (any direction) → R = mg
Case 3: Accelerating upward at a → R = m(g+a)
Case 4: Accelerating upward at a = g → R = 2mg
Case 5: Accelerating downward at a → R = m(g−a)
Case 6: Accelerating downward at a = g (free fall) → R = 0
Case 7: Accelerating downward at a > g → R is negative (not physically possible in free body; implies body leaves scale)
cards.example: NEET Trap: A lift decelerating while moving upward has the same formula as accelerating downward: R = m(g−a). Both give reduced apparent weight. Deceleration while moving up is equivalent to acceleration directed downward.

curriculum.hintText: Differences between the AP Physics and NEET approach to elevator problems.

gaps.title: 7-Case Table Format in NEET
gaps.description: NEET uses the NCERT Table 4.2 structure explicitly in questions: students are expected to have memorised all 7 cases. AP Physics derives results case-by-case from Newton’s Second Law applied to FBDs, which is correct but slower in an MCQ format.
bullets: Memorise all 7 cases by number so you can identify the correct formula instantly in 30 seconds.
For AP-background students: your FBD derivation skill is an asset — use it to derive any case you forget.

gaps.title: Weightlessness Conceptual Distinction
gaps.description: NEET specifically tests the definition: weightlessness = R = 0 (scale reads zero) even though actual weight mg still acts. AP Physics covers this but NEET MCQs explicitly distinguish weightlessness from zero gravity, which does not exist at Earth’s surface.
bullets: Weightlessness: astronauts in orbit, free-falling lift — R = 0 but actual weight mg is non-zero.
Zero gravity: only in deep space far from all masses. Not applicable near Earth’s surface.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is standing on a weighing machine in a lift. The tension in the rope holding the lift is 24 N (for the person; take g = 10 m/s²). Which is correct?

options.text: The lift is accelerating upward at 2 m/s²
The lift is moving down at constant speed
The lift is decelerating while moving up at 2 m/s²

questions.explanation: R = 24 N for m = 2 kg: R/m = 12 m/s². Since R = m(g+a) ⇒ 12 = 10 + a ⇒ a = 2 m/s² upward. The lift accelerates upward.

questions.question: When a lift is going up with uniform acceleration, a man standing in it weighs more. If the lift starts going down with the same uniform acceleration, then his weight compared to original will be:
questions.explanation: Going up at a: R = m(g+a) > mg. Going down at a: R = m(g−a) < mg. Since a is the same magnitude in both cases, the apparent weight going down is less than the apparent weight going up AND less than actual weight mg (assuming a < g).

questions.question: A person stands on a weighing scale in a lift. When the lift is stationary the scale reads 60 kg. When the lift is accelerating upward at 4 m/s², what does the scale read? (g = 10 m/s²)
In which of the following situations does a person experience weightlessness?
options: Lift moving up at constant speed

questions.question: A lift is going down with uniform acceleration. A man in it weighs 70% of his actual weight on a scale. The acceleration of the lift is (g = 10 m/s²):
When a lift moves down with acceleration equal to g, the apparent weight of a body inside it is:

items.title: Derive R = m(g±a) from FBD
items.description: Draw FBD with R (up), mg (down). Apply Newton’s second law. For upward acceleration: R − mg = ma ⇒ R = m(g+a). For downward: mg − R = ma ⇒ R = m(g−a).

items.title: Memorise all 7 cases in NCERT Table 4.2
items.description: List from memory: rest, constant velocity, accel up, a=g up, accel down, free fall, a>g down. Write the R formula for each.

items.title: Explain weightlessness conceptually
items.description: State that weightlessness means R = 0 (scale reads zero), not that mg = 0. The gravitational force still acts. Weightlessness occurs in free fall or orbit.

items.title: Identify deceleration scenario correctly
items.description: Deceleration while going up means net acceleration is downward: use R = m(g−a). This reduces apparent weight even though lift is moving upward.

items.question: What is actual weight vs apparent weight?
items.answer: Actual weight is the gravitational force W = mg. It is always the same for a given mass at a given location. Apparent weight (R) is the force exerted by the supporting surface (scale reading). R can be more, less, or equal to W depending on the acceleration of the lift.

items.question: Is weightlessness the same as being in zero gravity?
items.answer: No. Weightlessness means R = 0 (the surface exerts no normal force), which happens in free fall or orbit. Actual gravity g still acts on the person. Zero gravity strictly means g ≈ 0, which only occurs in deep space far from all massive bodies.

items.question: What happens when a > g downward?
items.answer: Mathematically R = m(g − a) becomes negative, which is physically impossible (a surface can only push, not pull). In reality the person leaves the scale surface and only gravity acts on them (free fall with the lift acting on them differently).

items.question: Why is apparent weight unchanged at constant velocity?
items.answer: At constant velocity, net acceleration = 0. Newton’s second law gives R − mg = 0, so R = mg. The speed of the lift (whether zero or 10 m/s upward or 5 m/s downward) does not matter; only acceleration determines the apparent weight.

grounding_summary: Page 195 introduces the setup: a body of mass m on a weighing machine inside a lift, with actual weight mg and apparent weight R (normal reaction).
Page 196 contains the complete NCERT Table 4.2 showing all 7 cases of lift acceleration and the corresponding apparent weight R for each case.
grounding_excerpt: When a body of mass m is placed on a weighing machine which is placed in a lift, then actual weight of the body is mg.
In this diagram the object of interest is isolated from its surroundings and the interactions between the object and the surroundings are represented in terms of forces.
Choose the axes and write equation of motion.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Primary source for the 7-condition apparent weight table (NCERT Table 4.2); derivation of R = m(g+a) and R = m(g−a) and the weightlessness case R = 0 are all from NCERT Chapter 4.

sources.title: Laws of Motion Detailed Explanation — Physics Class 11 NEET
sources.why_used: Confirms the 7-case lift table as a high-frequency NEET question source, provides supplementary solved examples and PYQ-style question patterns for apparent weight problems.

review.notes: Grounded in pages 195-196. The 7-case table is from NCERT Table 4.2 as reproduced in the OCR markdown. All formulas and derivations are within NEET syllabus scope.
